heroTitle: Start with the Tamil words you hear everywhere.
heroText: This quick-start lesson focuses on common words, not full phrases, so learners can recognize the little building blocks that show up again and again in family conversation.
panelList: Question words, time words, and quick response words
Amount words like a little, more, very, and enough
Core home and family words you will hear constantly
Simple action roots that show up in many spoken patterns
title: Quick-start Tamil words
description: This lesson is a warm-up. Instead of asking learners to produce full sentences right away, it helps them get familiar with the small words that make home Tamil feel less overwhelming.

studySection.title: Learn the words you keep hearing
studySection.description: Tap a word card to hear it, see the meaning, and get comfortable with the question words, time words, family words, and action roots that glue conversations together.

audio.note: This localhost lesson is wired for ElevenLabs-generated audio. Configure ELEVENLABS_API_KEY and ELEVENLABS_VOICE_ID in .env.local, then run serve.py.

phrases.useCase: A very common yes in home Tamil.
phrases.note: This is the kind of everyday word learners will hear constantly.

phrases.useCase: Used for no, not, or absence depending on context.
phrases.note: This one word does a lot of work in spoken Tamil.

phrases.useCase: A common response word in all kinds of home situations.
phrases.note: Short reaction words like this help learners sound more natural quickly.

phrases.useCase: Appears in many common questions later in the course.
phrases.note: Learners do not need a full sentence yet. Just recognizing this word is useful.

phrases.useCase: Shows up in questions about people, callers, and visitors.
phrases.note: A very useful people-focused question word.

phrases.useCase: Shows up in questions like where you are going or where something is.
phrases.note: This becomes much easier to notice once the learner has heard it on its own.

phrases.useCase: A high-frequency timing word in family conversation.
phrases.note: This word matters because so many home questions are really about time.

phrases.useCase: Useful in day-to-day check-ins about what happened today.
phrases.note: This will come back strongly in the lesson about talking about your day.

phrases.useCase: A common planning word for family logistics and future plans.
phrases.note: Useful early because families often ask about tomorrow and later.

phrases.useCase: Useful in many replies and requests.
phrases.note: A small but powerful word that appears everywhere in spoken Tamil.

phrases.useCase: Useful when delaying something or talking about what comes next.
phrases.note: This helps learners catch the general idea even when they miss the rest of a sentence.

phrases.useCase: Shows up when talking about more food, more time, or something not finished yet.
phrases.note: This one is especially useful because it can signal either time or quantity.

phrases.useCase: Used in requests, amounts, and softening speech.
phrases.note: This is one of the most useful single words in the whole app.

phrases.useCase: Shows up in compliments, complaints, and explanations.
phrases.note: Great for catching emotional intensity in a sentence.

phrases.useCase: Useful in broad statements about food, things, or people.
phrases.note: Learners will hear this in many family-style summaries.

phrases.useCase: Useful with food, serving, and many everyday home situations.
phrases.note: This is a tiny word with a very big payoff.

phrases.useCase: A key word for saying what you want later in the course.
phrases.note: This shows up in many useful beginner patterns.

phrases.useCase: A common response when you do not know an answer.
phrases.note: This is technically more than a root, but it is heard as one very common spoken unit.

phrases.useCase: Useful when you heard Tamil but did not understand the meaning.
phrases.note: A high-value survival word for heritage learners.

phrases.useCase: A very high-frequency everyday noun.
phrases.note: This is one of the easiest practical words to recognize early.

phrases.useCase: One of the most common home nouns in family Tamil.
phrases.note: This helps later dinner-table lessons feel much more familiar.

phrases.useCase: Shows up when talking about going home or someone else's house.
phrases.note: This is a core family-conversation word.

phrases.useCase: Very useful in everyday leaving-the-house situations.
phrases.note: A small practical word with immediate payoff.

phrases.useCase: One of the first family words many learners already half-recognize.
phrases.note: Useful to anchor the lesson in family relationships.

phrases.useCase: A very common family word that will fit many later scenes.
phrases.note: Another good anchor word for heritage learners.

phrases.useCase: Useful inside family context and also sometimes as a respectful address outside the family.
phrases.note: This word carries both family and social usefulness.

phrases.useCase: Useful for family situations and polite everyday address.
phrases.note: This is another word learners may hear inside and outside the home.

phrases.useCase: Useful for heritage learners who want to talk to older relatives.
phrases.note: This helps the lesson feel more culturally grounded.

phrases.useCase: Useful for family-focused learners talking to older relatives.
phrases.note: This fits the same family-relationship set as paati.

phrases.useCase: Shows up in common home invitations and calls.
phrases.note: A tiny action word that appears in many spoken patterns.

phrases.useCase: Useful as a base action word and for recognizing other go-forms later.
phrases.note: Even when learners do not produce it yet, hearing it early helps.

phrases.useCase: A high-frequency root behind many food-related spoken forms.
phrases.note: This is one of the most important action roots in the home context.

phrases.useCase: Useful in common spoken instructions and reactions.
phrases.note: This helps learners get used to short action-root forms.

phrases.useCase: Appears in many useful spoken requests later in the app.
phrases.note: A very important speech-related action root.

phrases.useCase: A core root behind many requests for food, water, or objects.
phrases.note: This will make request forms easier to recognize later.
